Town of
Corte Madera

300 TAMALPAIS DRIVE AT WILLOW AVENUE
P.O. BOX 159, CORTE MADERA, CA 94925-0159

June 4, 1992

Deanne Stone, Esq.
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Formal Written Advice regarding Councilmember Vaso
Medigovich, Town of Corte Madera

Official for whom opinion is solicited: Vaso
Medigovich, Councilmember of the Town of Corte Madera.

Address of Mr. Medigovich: 160 Birch Drive, Corte
Madera, CA 94976

Dear Ms. Stone:

I am the Town Attorney for the Town of Corte Madera. I have
been authorized and requested by Vaso Medigovich, one of the Town's
Councilmembers, to solicit this formal written advice from the
FPPC. This request is submitted pursuant to 2 Cal. Code Regs.
section 18329(b).

Factual Statement

The Town of Corte Madera is located in the County of Marin.
It is a general law city. Its council is made up of five
individuals, one of whom is Vaso Medigovich.

Mr. Medigovich owns four separate pieces of real property
located in the Town: one is his residence and the other three are
rental properties which he rents to other individuals. The
locations and addresses of these four parcels are shown on the
attached, color-coded plan of the Town of Corte Madera. See
Exhibit "A".

An abandoned railroad right-of-way ("ROW") is located to the
north of these four parcels. It is partly within the City of
Larkspur and partly in the Town of Corte Madera ("Town"). Its
location is shown on Exhibit "A". It is owned by the Porter-Cooley
family.

Deanne Stone, Esq.
June 4, 1992
Page 2

     The Town staff and the Porter-Cooley family have been
discussing the uses to which the Porter-Cooley family would like to
put this ROW. In a staff report to the Council dated June 11, 1991
(for a council meeting of June 18, 1991) the various parts of this
ROW are depicted and a description of the proposed uses for each
part is set forth. Please see Exhibit "B", pages 2-3. You will
note that in this staff report, reference is made to a general plan
amendment. That amendment has already been approved and is not the
subject of this request for advice.

     However, as you can appreciate from reading the description of
the proposed elements of the arrangement between the Town and
Porter-Cooley, a multitude of decisions by the Town Council must be
made to effect this proposal. Be mindful, however, that no
decisions other than the general plan amendment have been made to
approve any of the land use projects described in Exhibit "B".
Thus, for example, as to the Tamal Vista West segment of the ROW,
in order for the Porter-Cooley family to develop this land into
the proposed 14 unit housing development, a subdivision map,
preliminary plans, design review approval and perhaps a rezone
application, would have to be filed by Porter-Cooley and approved
by the Town. Indeed, the landowners may decide that a development
agreement is the best vehicle to implement their development
scheme, and, naturally, that development agreement would require
Council approval. Furthermore, Town purchase of a portion or all
of the ROW is or may be suggested.

     In short, a variety of land use and other decisions will have
to be made to consummate the Porter-Cooley proposal.

Advice Requested

     The question for which I am, in behalf of Vaso Medigovich,
requesting advice is: whether or not, under the Fair Political
Practices Act and its implementing regulations, Mr. Medigovich has
a conflict of interest in connection with such future decisions
because of his ownership interest in the four pieces of real
property described and shown in Exhibit "A".

     In this connection, please be advised that Mr. Medigovich has
retained the opinion of a real estate appraiser whose resume and

jw/vaso1

Deanne Stone, Esq.
June 4, 1992
Page 3

opinions are attached hereto as Exhibits C". That appraiser has
opined that any development on the Porter-Cooley property, and
particularly that described in Exhibit "B", will have no effect on
the four parcels owned by Mr. Medigovich.

Your timely response to this request for formal written advice
would be greatly appreciated.

Sincerely yours,

JEFFREY A. WALTER, a p.c.

[signature]

JEFFREY A. WALTER

JAW/kp

CORTE MADERA TOWN COUNCIL
STAFF REPORT

ITEM NO. 5

REPORT DATE: JUNE 11, 1991
MEETING DATE: JUNE 18, 1991

TO: TOWN MANAGER, MAYOR, AND MEMBERS OF THE TOWN COUNCIL

FROM: DAVID HALE, PLANNING DIRECTOR

SUBJECT: GENERAL PLAN AMENDMENT NO. 91-01 -- PORTER/COOLEY
PROPERTY

RECOMMENDATION:

Conduct the public hearing and then adopt a resolution to:

A. Accept the Environmental Impact Report certified for the
1989 General Plan as the environmental document relied
upon for this proposed amendment.

B. Amend the General Plan's Figure 2, General Plan Diagram,
to reflect the following land use designations: "Multi-
family Residential" between the High Canal and Tamal
Vista Boulevard; "Office" on the property northerly 400 feet of
the "V" at Industrial Way. with cable and equestrian
trails from the High Canal easterly to Industrial Way.

C. Place an asterisk on Figure 2 for the property described
in the preceding paragraph referring to a Specific Site
Policy as follows:

2.5.n. Any private development proposed to be
undertaken on the former Northwestern Pacific
Railroad right-of-way, now owned by the
Porter/Cooley family, shall only be undertaken
as part of a Development Agreement between the
Town and the property owners.

D. Add the following sentence to existing Policy 3.2.n.:

The wetland buffer for any project along the
former Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-
way easterly of Highway 101 may be less than
100 feet, but such projects shall provide the
maximum feasible buffer, to be determined in
consultation with regulatory agencies at the
time any project is proposed.

EXHIBIT B

□ THOMAS

SUMMARY:

This proposed General Plan amendment continues the discussions
held by the Planning Commission and Town Council during the
General Plan hearing in 1989. Throughout the hearings on the
General Plan then, the Town and the property owners wanted to
resolve the land use issues relating to the former
Northeastern Pacific Railroad right-of-way, extending from
Williams Avenue in the City of Larkspur easterly beyond U.S.
Highway 101 where the property forms a "Y" around Number
Marsh, a wetland owned by the Town. For illustration
purposes, the sketch below shows the major components of the
proposed General Plan amendment. The numbers on the sketch
correspond to the brief descriptions below the sketch.

[THIS IS FIGURE: A sketch map showing the layout of different sections labeled 1-5, with directional markers for Larkspur Boundary, North arrow, and various roads/features marked. The sketch includes labels for "High Canal", "Tamal Vista Blvd", "U.S. 101/Wilson Ln", "Larkspur", and "Industrial Way".]

1. Larkspur segment. The segment along the southerly boundary of Redwood High School is actually
outside the boundaries of Corte Madera. The property owners propose to deed this land to the
Town of Corte Madera. The property is 100 feet wide, almost one-half a mile in length, and
contains an elevated levy with the publically used gravel trail on top. If the property is
deeded to Corte Madera, the Town would probably desire to petition LAFCO to transfer that
property into the Town of Corte Madera, although such an action would not be required. The
linear strip would provide one potential for a future trail connection between and Watershed and
the property, as it is located within the City of Larkspur. No General Plan amendment is proposed for this portion of
the property, as it is located within the City of Larkspur.

2. Tamal Vista West. This property extends from the High Canal to Tamal Vista Boulevard. The
proposal is to amend the General Plan to provide a land use designation with a potential zoning
density of up to 10.9 dwelling units per acre. The property owners propose 14 units of single-
family, attached housing on this site. The property contains 56,000 square feet and a
schematic design has been prepared to show the feasibility of this density. As part of the
proposed development on this site, the developer would be required to construct a pedestrian
and bicyclist bridge over the High Canal to link with the property to the east, which is a de
facto trail in Larkspur. This trail, where it traverses the Tamal Vista West site, would be
designed and developed by the property owner on a 20-foot pathway easement to be deeded to the
Town.

2

3.    Tamal Vista Boulevard right-of-way. The property owners contend that Tamal Vista Boulevard
was never deeded to the Town and that fee simple ownership of this land is held by the
Porter/Cooley families. The property owners have agreed to deed this right-of-way to the Town
and thereby clear any questions which might cloud the title or create legal issues regarding
public use of this roadway.

4.    Tamal Vista East. This segment provides the opportunity for the Town to develop a needed east-
west connection under the freeway. Although the present General Plan designation, Maricela W
would not be changed, the property owners propose to sell the land to the Town at 50 percent
of its appraised fair market value, with the total cost to the Town not to exceed $50,000 -
an offer extending through December 31, 1991. The Town Council would be unable to
entitle to negotiate in the date with the applicants which would be included in a future
Development Agreement. The appraisal would be based on these assumptions: allowed land use, Zoning restrictions as contained in the M - Light Industrial District, a 20-
foot pathway assessment would be deeded to the Town, consideration would be used, and there would be no severance damages included in the appraisal.
The Town would select and pay for the appraiser. If the property owners disagree with the
appraisal, the owners will retain their appraiser; if there is no agreement between the
two appraisals, those two appraisers will select a third appraiser as arbitier. After December
31, the "50 percent off" arrangements would expire, and the owners would then add 10 percent
of the established price per year thereafter.

This potential for an arterial to connect Redwood Highway and Nellen Avenue to Tamal Vista
Boulevard would result in a significant improvement in the level of service for several of the
most congested intersections in the Town.

5.    "Y" shaped parcel. The General Plan land use designation for this piece would be "Office" for
the 600 feet approaching Industrial Way. Under this designation, the property owner would be
allowed to develop an office building containing 12,000 square feet of gross floor area to be
located at the northerly end of the "Y". As part of the overall conceptual parameters of the
owners' proposal, the owners will deed to the Town a 20-foot easement for a pathway through
this property, and they will consent to develop that pathway on the office parcel as part of
the remainder of the "Y" property would be deeded to the Town, over the access from Industrial Way to the north-south alignment of the former Northwestern
Pacific Railroad right-of-way.

CEQA STATUS:

Guidelines implementing the California Environmental
Quality Act allow the use of an earlier Environmental
Impact Report as the environmental document for a
subsequent project if the environmental circumstances are
essentially the same. The EIR prepared for the General
Plan and certified in December 1989 actually considered
the land use relationships being proposed at this time.
The draft General Plan had proposed the higher density
residential development on the west side of Tamal Vista
Boulevard, but this proposal was revised during the final
hearings on the General Plan. The EIR actually included
an evaluation of this higher residential density without
finding significant impacts. Also, the EIR contemplated
the development of 66,000 square feet of office space at
nearby Madera Bay Park. Thus, in a sense, the
applicants' potential proposal for this 12,000 square

3

feet of office development could be substituted because
of its much smaller scale.

An initial study has been undertaken for this General
Plan amendment, and it shows that conditions in the area
have not changed materially since the 1989 EIR was
prepared. In preparing the initial study, the over-
riding concern was what changes have been made since the
earlier certified EIR was drafted.

Citizens' review. Public notice of this proposed General
Plan Amendment included the notice of staffs and the
Planning Commission's recommendation of the earlier EIR
as the environmental document for this project. One
response was sent which might be interpreted as a comment
on the use of this EIR. That response was received from
Barbara Salzman, speaking for the Marin Audubon Society,
who made the following statement: "It [a he proposed
amendment] would set the stage for and, in fact, require
that this specific project be carried out by a
development agreement, yet the environmental review and
analysis of potential impacts has been non-existent."
Mrs. Salzman's full letter is attached for your Council's
review. A response was given to Mrs. Salzman's letter
on the day it was received at the Planning Commission
meeting, explaining that a general environmental
evaluation has already been given in the earlier EIR;
however, a thorough analysis will be required at the time
any specific development proposal is submitted.

The 1989 EIR included the same trail system which is
proposed by this application. Mrs. Salzman's concerns
about the design for such a trail are important concerns
but can more easily be addressed once a specific project
is proposed. For example, no design has been proposed
for the trails; no surface material has been proposed;
and no proposals for mitigations have been made.

Recommended CEQA action. Because there have been no
material changes brought about by the proposed amendment,
it would be appropriate to rely on that EIR for this
project, which staff and the Planning Commission are
recommending. At the time specific development is
proposed, the EIR prepared at that time will focus on
environmental impacts of the specific
development proposal and appropriate mitigation measures
for the project.

1 It should be emphasized that the underlying development proposals described herein are
but mere concepts. No development applications, no precise plans, and no design
plans have been submitted to the Town for consideration.

4

BACKGROUND:

As a general description of the overall history of the
land uses for this former right-of-way, it can be said
that property as to Tamal Vista Boulevard, the land is now
in private ownership.
The present owners are
contemplating developing portions of their property and
have expressed a desire to offer certain benefits to the
Town.

Discussions of land uses for the Porter/Cooley property
took place during the General Plan hearings in 1989. At
the conclusion of those hearings, the Town Council
designated the Porter/Cooley property as Low Density
Residential, with the exception of the portion easterly
of Tamal Vista Boulevard which is designated for Arterial
use, and the area easterly of U.S. Highway 101, which
is designated Wetlands, Unique Marshland, Related
Habitat.

After the adoption of the General Plan in December 1989,
the Town Council appointed a committee to work with the
property owners to attempt to work out a reasonable
resolution to the land use issues on this property. The
committee consisted of Councilmen Richardson and Wolff
and Planning Commissioner Wheelock. After numerous
meetings of the Committee, the ingredients of a possible
General Plan amendment evolved, concurred in by a
majority of the Committee members but not unanimously.
The applicants then incorporated the ingredients of a
potential land use solution into a General Plan amendment
application which was submitted by the property owners
in early 1990. That General Plan amendment was
considered by the Planning Commission and recommended to
your Town Council along with 17 other amendment
proposals. Your Council took no action on the
Porter/Cooley portion of the General Plan amendment due
to its complexity and suggested that the matter be
considered as a separate General Plan amendment. The
matter is now back before the Town in generally the same
configuration, reflecting the elements of the Committee
proposed resolution in 1990. The primary difference is
the recommendation now that the General Plan contain
language requiring that any private development
be undertaken only as part of an overall Development
Agreement.

Fairly wide support has been expressed by citizens of the
Town for the proposed arrangements in land use.
Objections have been voiced by the Marin Audubon Society
in a letter from Barbara Salzman which is attached. Much

5

of her concerns will appropriately be addressed in the
Environmental Impact Report which will be required for
the project specific proposals. Residents of the Casa
Madera development adjacent to this property along Tamal
Vista Boulevard also expressed objections or concerns at
the Planning Commission public hearing because of the
potential for additional housing units at that location.


STAFF ANALYSIS OF THE OVER-ALL PROPOSAL

Town and Property Owner Benefits. The proposals
contained in the General Plan Amendment application would
accommodate the recommendations made by the Committee
that studied this matter in 1990. The Committee gave a
lot of thought to a proposal that would result in
significant public benefits and at the same time be
attractive enough to the land owner to make the proposal
workable. The significant benefits to the Town are: 1)
the opportunity to create a linear trail that could
eventually link the Village Square area with a trail
system running through Corte Madera and north through
Larkspur; 2) the ability to acquire the right-of-way for
a Nellen Avenue extension, providing a much needed east-
west arterial connection in this part of Town and 3) the
potential for providing a bicycle path connecting the
east side of Corte Madera with Redwood High School with
only a couple of street crossings. The significant
advantages of this proposal to the property owner are:
1) the increase in density allowed on the Tamal Vista
West parcel, which is now limited to a maximum of two
dwelling units; 2) cash payment for the land the Town
would acquire for the Nellen Avenue extension; and 3) the
ability to develop an office building on land now
designated in the General Plan as Open Space.

It should be emphasized, however, that the descriptions
of the owners' proposal contained herein are general in
nature and are not being presented at this time as formal
land use applications. The descriptions are somewhat
detailed and included here so that the Town Council will
have a general idea of what the owners envision for the
subject parcels.

General Plan Conflict. The proposed office development
and the trail along the northerly leg of the "Y" create
a potential conflict with Policy 3.2.n., which states:

3.2.n. Establish and maintain buffers of at least
100 feet between marshland and adjacent uses where

6

habitat values would be protected and where the
buffer would not eliminate all economically viable
use of the property.

That policy as now written would restrict the proposed
enlargement of that area. Staff recommends that a
specific policy be prepared to allow less than the 100-
foot buffer for the "H" segment, as proposed in the
Planning Commission's recommendation.

Environmental evaluation. Approval of this General Plan
amendment enables the applicant to proceed with more
detailed design studies and the Environment Impact Report
which would be required for the proposed projects. A new
development plans than now when the proposal is still in
a concept phase.

Development Agreement. The details of this proposed
General Plan amendment should be carried out in a
Development Agreement to be entered into by the property
owners and the Town Council. The General Plan amendment,
therefore, would change the designations on the land use
diagram to accommodate the applicants' proposals, and
there would be an asterisk on the diagram to refer to a
specific site policy requiring a Development Agreement
for any future private development of this property.
This approach protects the property owners and the Town
by ensuring that the proposals described herein can be
carried out.


ATTACHMENTS:

1. Draft resolution.
2. Letter from Marin Audubon Society, dated May 27, 1991.
3. Letter from Kathleen Hartzell, Neil Cummins PTA, May 28, 1991.
4. Letter from Cathy Lane, Hall Middle School PTA, May 28, 1991.
5. Letter from David Petterson, 400 Tamal Plaza, June 11, 1991.
6. Letter from Sandra Y. Marker, The Railroad Open Space
   Preservation Group, June 12, 1991.
7. Letter from Anne Stranczek, June 12, 1991.


cc: Applicants

Mr. Vaso Medigovich

Corte Madera, CA 94925

Dear Mr. Medigovich,

You asked me, a professional real estate appraiser,
for an opinion as to whether or not certain development
would have any valuation effect on properties you own
nearby.

The specific development is that proposed for the old
railroad R/W northerly of Birch Drive that proceeds easterly
to the freeway.

This is a proposed bikeway and various other components
described in the staff report for the Corte Madera Town
Council meeting of June 18, 1991, General Plan Amendment
#91-01.

Your property at 17 Lakeside is approximately 0.5 mile
from the R/W and 47 Lakeside is approximately 0.3 mile.
Your property at 30 Blue Rock Court is approximately 0.3
mile by crow and 0.5 mile by street.

It is my opinion that development of the above R/W
would have no effect on the value of any of the above properties. Furthermore, such development would not influence
rental value of these properties.

Very truly yours,

                                    June 2, 1991


Mr. Vaso Medigovich
160 Birch Avenue
Corte Madera, CA 94925

Dear Mr. Medigovich:

     You requested that I inspect the former 100'
wide railroad R/W that parallels Birch Avenue and
adjoins homes on the north side of Birch Avenue.
This is known now as the Porter-Cooley property.

     The purpose of this inspection was to determine
from real estate appraisal perspective whether devel-
opment of this property could in any way affect the
value of your home at 160 Birch Avenue.

     Your home is separated from this land by Birch
Avenue and the line of homes across the street from
your home. The southerly boundary is approximately
150 feet from your front property line.

     The Porter-Cooley property has a trail on its
raised portion which is approximately 10' above the
street elevation. Your home cannot be seen from this
trail. Should residential development occur on this
portion of the Porter-Cooley property and even if it
were visible from your property, the visual effect
should have no more bearing on valuing your property
than that of homes across the street.

     If the property were used for a street, it would
have no effect on the value of your property.

     In summary, development of the Porter-Cooley
property will have no effect whatsoever on the value
of your residence at 160 Birch Avenue.

                                    Very truly yours,

                                    [signature]
                                    W. T. Ashby
                                    Real Estate Appraiser
                                    135 Golden Hind Passage
                                    Corte Madera, CA 94925

sections.conclusion:
This proposed General Plan amendment continues the discussions held by the Planning Commission and Town Council during the General Plan hearing in 1989. Throughout the hearings on the General Plan then, the Town and the property owners wanted to resolve the land use issues relating to the former Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way, extending from Williams Avenue in the City of Larkspur easterly beyond U.S. Highway 101 where the property forms a "Y" around Humber Marsh, a wetland owned by the Town. For illustration purposes, the sketch below shows the major components of the proposed General Plan amendment. The numbers on the sketch correspond to the brief descriptions below the sketch.

![Sketch showing Larkspur boundary, Tamal Vista Blvd., High Canal, US Highway 101, Industrial Way, and former NWPRR ROW](https://i.imgur.com/3Q5z5QG.png)

1. **Larkspur segment.** The segment along the southerly boundary of Redwood High School is actually outside the boundaries of Corte Madera. The property owners propose to deed this land to the Town of Corte Madera. The property is 100 feet wide, almost one-half mile in length, and contains the elevated levy with the publicly used gravel trail on top. If the property is deeded to Corte Madera, the Town would probably desire to petition LAFCO to transfer that property into the Town of Corte Madera, although such an action would not be required. The linear strip would provide the potential for a future trail connection beyond Larkspur and southerly to the Village Square. No General Plan amendment is proposed for this portion of the property, as it is located within the City of Larkspur.

2. **Tamal Vista West.** This property extends from the High Canal to Tamal Vista Boulevard. The proposal is to amend the General Plan to provide a land use designation with a potential zoning density of up to 10.9 dwelling units per acre. The property owners propose 14 units of single-family, attached housing on this site. The property contains 56,000 square feet and a schematic design has been prepared to show the feasibility of this density. As part of the proposed development on this site, the developer would be required to construct a pedestrian and bicyclist bridge over the high canal to link with the property to the east, which is a de facto trail in Larkspur. This trail, where it traverses the Tamal Vista West site, would be designed and developed by the property owner on a 20-foot pathway easement to be deeded to the Town.

3. Tamal Vista Boulevard right-of-way. The property owners contend that Tamal Vista Boulevard was never deeded to the Town and that fee simple ownership of this land is held by the Porter/Cooley families. The property owners have agreed to deed this right-of-way to the Town and thereby clear any questions which might cloud the title or create legal issues regarding public use of this roadway.

4. Tamal Vista East. This segment provides the opportunity for the Town to develop a needed east-west connection under the freeway. Although the present General Plan designation, "Arterial," would not be changed, the property owners propose to sell the land to the Town at 50 percent of its appraised fair market value, with the total cost to the Town not to exceed $350,000 - an offer extending through December 31, 1991. The Town Council would be the appropriate entity to negotiate this date with the applicants which would be included in a future Development Agreement. The appraisal would be based on these assumptions: Industrial as the allowed land use, Zoning restrictions as contained in the M-Light Industrial District, a 20-foot pathway easement would be deeded to the Town, and the appraisal approach used for condemnation would be used, and there would be no severance damages included in the appraisal. The Town would select and pay for the appraiser. If the property owners disagree with the Town's appraisal, the owners will retain their appraiser; if there is no agreement between the two appraisals, those two appraisers will select a third appraiser as arbiter. After December 31, the "50 percent off" arrangement would expire, and the owners would then add 10 percent of the established price per year thereafter.

This potential for an arterial to connect Redwood Highway and Nellen Avenue to Tamal Vista Boulevard would result in a significant improvement in the Level of Service for several of the most congested intersections in the Town.

5. "Y" shaped parcel. The General Plan land use designation for this piece would be "Office" for the 400 feet approaching Industrial Way. Under this designation, the property owner would be allowed to develop an office building containing 12,000 square feet of gross floor area to be located at the northerly end of the "Y". As part of the overall conceptual parameters of the owners' proposal, the owners will deed to the Town a 20-foot easement for a pathway through this property, and they will commit to develop that pathway on the office parcel as part of that future development. The remainder of the "Y" property would be deeded to the Town, including the holding at the end of Industrial Way which would provide the Town with control over the access from Industrial Way to the north-south alignment of the former Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way.

CEQA STATUS:

Guidelines implementing the California Environmental Quality Act allow the use of an earlier Environmental Impact Report as the environmental document for a subsequent project if the environmental circumstances are essentially the same. The EIR prepared for the General Plan and certified in December 1989 actually considered the land use relationships being proposed at this time. The draft General Plan had proposed the higher density residential development on the west side of Tamal Vista Boulevard, but this proposal was revised during the final hearings on the General Plan. The EIR actually included an evaluation of this higher residential density without finding significant impacts. Also, the EIR contemplated the development of 66,000 square feet of office space at nearby Madera Bay Park. Thus, in a sense, the applicants' potential proposal for this 12,000 square

feet of office development could be substituted because of its much smaller scale.

An initial study has been undertaken for this General Plan amendment, and it shows that conditions in the area have not changed materially since the 1989 EIR was prepared. In preparing the initial study, the overriding concern was what changes have been made since the earlier certified EIR was drafted.

Citizens' review. Public notice of this proposed General Plan Amendment included the notice of staff's and the Planning Commission's recommendation of the earlier EIR as the environmental document for this project. One response was sent which might be interpreted as a comment on the use of this EIR. That response was received from Barbara Salzman, speaking for the Marin Audubon Society, who made the following statement: "It [i.e. the proposed amendment] would set the stage for and, in fact, require that this specific project be carried out by a development agreement, yet the environmental review and analysis of potential impacts has been non-existent." Mrs. Salzman's full letter is attached for your Council's review. A response was given to Mrs. Salzman's letter on the day it was received at the Planning Commission meeting, explaining that a general environmental evaluation has already been given in the earlier EIR; however, a thorough analysis will be required at the time any specific development proposal is submitted.

The 1989 EIR included the same trail system which is proposed by this application. Mrs. Salzman's concerns about the design for such a trail are important concerns but can more easily be addressed once a specific project is proposed. For example, no design has been proposed for the trails; no surface material has been proposed; and no proposals for mitigations have been made.

Recommended CEQA action. Because there have been no material changes brought about by the proposed amendment, it would be appropriate to rely on that EIR for this project, which staff and the Planning Commission are recommending. At the time specific development is proposed, the EIR prepared at that time will focus on specific environmental impacts of the specific development proposal and appropriate mitigation measures for the project.1

1 It should be emphasized that the underlying development proposals described herein are but mere concepts. No development applications, no precise plans, no project plans, and no design plans have been submitted to the Town for consideration.
sections.facts: As a general description of the overall history of the land uses for this former right-of-way, it can be said that, except as to Tamal Vista Boulevard, the land is now in private ownership. The present owners are contemplating developing portions of their property and have expressed a desire to offer certain benefits to the Town.
sections.analysis:
s of land uses for the Porter/Cooley property took place during the General Plan hearings in 1989. At the conclusion of those hearings, the Town Council designated the Porter/Cooley property as Low Density Residential, with the exception of the portion easterly of Tamal Vista Boulevard which is designated for Arterial use, and the area easterly of U.S. Highway 101, which is designated Wetlands, Unique Marshland, Related Habitat.

After the adoption of the General Plan in December 1989, the Town Council appointed a committee to work with the property owners to attempt to work out a reasonable resolution to the land use issues on this property. The committee consisted of Councilmen Richardson and Wolff and Planning Commissioner Wheelock. After numerous meetings of the Committee, the ingredients of a possible General Plan amendment evolved, concurred in by a majority of the Committee members but not unanimously. The applicants then incorporated the ingredients of a potential land use solution into a General Plan amendment application which was submitted by the property owners in early 1990. That General Plan amendment was considered by the Planning Commission and recommended to your Town Council along with 17 other amendment proposals. Your Council took no action on the Porter/Cooley portion of the General Plan amendment due to its complexity and suggested that the matter be considered as a separate General Plan amendment. The matter is now back before the Town in generally the same configuration, reflecting the elements of the Committee's proposed resolution in 1990. The primary difference is the recommendation now that the General Plan contain actual language requiring that any private development be undertaken only as part of an overall Development Agreement.

Fairly wide support has been expressed by citizens of the Town for the proposed arrangements in land use. Objections have been voiced by the Marin Audubon Society in a letter from Barbara Salzman which is attached. Much

of her concerns will appropriately be addressed in the Environmental Impact Report which will be required for the project specific proposals. Residents of the Casa Madera development adjacent to this property along Tamal Vista Boulevard also expressed objections or concerns at the Planning Commission public hearing because of the potential for additional housing units at that location.

STAFF ANALYSIS OF THE OVER-ALL PROPOSAL

Town and Property Owner Benefits. The proposals contained in the General Plan Amendment application would accommodate the recommendations made by the Committee that studied this matter in 1990. The Committee gave a lot of thought to a proposal that would result in significant public benefits and at the same time be attractive enough to the land owner to make the proposal workable. The significant benefits to the Town are: 1) the opportunity to create a linear trail that could eventually link the Village Square area with a trail system running through Corte Madera and north through Larkspur; 2) the ability to acquire the right-of-way for a Nellen Avenue extension, providing a much needed east-west arterial connection in this part of Town; and 3) the potential for providing a bicycle path connecting the east side of Corte Madera with Redwood High School with only a couple of street crossings. The significant advantages of this proposal to the property owner are: 1) the increase in density allowed on the Tamal Vista West parcel, which is now limited to a maximum of two dwelling units; 2) cash payment for the land the Town would acquire for the Nellen Avenue extension; and 3) the ability to develop an office building on land now designated in the General Plan as Open Space.

It should be emphasized, however, that the descriptions of the owners' proposal contained herein are general in nature and are not being presented at this time as formal land use applications. The descriptions are somewhat detailed and included here so that the Town Council will have a general idea of what the owners envision for the subject parcels.

General Plan Conflict. The proposed office development and the trail along the northerly leg of the "Y" create a potential conflict with Policy 3.2.n., which states:

3.2.n. Establish and maintain buffers of at least 100 feet between marshland and adjacent uses where

habitat values would be protected and where the buffer would not eliminate all economically viable use of the property.

That policy as now written would restrict the proposed development of that area. Staff recommends that a specific policy be prepared to allow less than the 100-foot buffer for the "Y" segment, as proposed in the Planning Commission's recommendation.

Environmental evaluation. Approval of this General Plan amendment enables the applicant to proceed with more detailed design studies and the Environment Impact Report which would be required for the proposed projects. A new EIR would be more appropriate as part of the specific development plans than now when the proposal is still in a concept phase.

Development Agreement. The details of this proposed General Plan amendment should be carried out in a Development Agreement to be entered into by the property owners and the Town Council. The General Plan amendment, therefore, would change the designations on the land use diagram to accommodate the applicants' proposals, and there would be an asterisk on the diagram to refer to a specific site policy requiring a Development Agreement for any future private development of this property. This approach protects the property owners and the Town by ensuring that the proposals described herein can be carried out.

ATTACHMENTS:

1. Draft resolution.
2. Letter from Marin Audubon Society, dated May 27, 1991.
3. Letter from Kathleen Hartzell, Neil Cummins PTA, May 28, 1991.
4. Letter from Cathy Lane, Hall Middle School PTA, May 28, 1991.
5. Letter from David Petterson, 400 Tamal Plaza, June 11, 1991.
6. Letter from Sandra Y. Marker, The Railroad Open Space Preservation Group, June 12, 1991.
7. Letter from Anne Stranczek, June 12, 1991.

cc: Applicants

June 17, 1991

Mr. Vaso Medigovich  
160 Birch Avenue  
Corte Madera, CA 94925

Dear Mr. Medigovich,

You asked me, a professional real estate appraiser, for an opinion as to whether or not certain development would have any valuation effect on properties you own nearby.

The specific development is that proposed for the old railroad R/W northerly of Birch Drive that proceeds easterly to the freeway.

This is a proposed bikeway and various other components described in the staff report for the Corte Madera Town Council meeting of June 18, 1991, General Plan Amendment #91-01.

Your property at 17 Lakeside is approximately 0.5 mile from the R/W and 47 Lakeside is approximately 0.3 mile. Your property at 30 Blue Rock Court is approximately 0.3 mile by crow and 0.5 mile by street.

It is my opinion that development of the above R/W would have no effect on the value of any of the above properties. Furthermore, such development would not influence rental value of these properties.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: This proposed General Plan amendment continues the discussions held by the Planning Commission and Town Council during the General Plan hearing in 1989. Throughout the hearings on the General Plan then, the Town and the property owners wanted to resolve the land use issues relating to the former Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way, extending from Williams Avenue in the City of Larkspur easterly beyond U.S. Highway 101 where the property forms a "Y" around Humber Marsh, a wetland owned by the Town. For illustration purposes, the sketch below shows the major components of the proposed General Plan amendment. The numbers on the sketch correspond to the brief descriptions below the sketch.

![Sketch showing Larkspur boundary, Tamal Vista Blvd., High Canal, US Highway 101, Industrial Way, and former NWPRR ROW](https://i.imgur.com/3Q5z5QG.png)

1. **Larkspur segment.** The segment along the southerly boundary of Redwood High School is actually outside the boundaries of Corte Madera. The property owners propose to deed this land to the Town of Corte Madera. The property is 100 feet wide, almost one-half mile in length, and contains the elevated levy with the publicly used gravel trail on top. If the property is deeded to Corte Madera, the Town would probably desire to petition LAFCO to transfer that property into the Town of Corte Madera, although such an action would not be required. The linear strip would provide the potential for a future trail connection beyond Larkspur and southerly to the Village Square. No General Plan amendment is proposed for this portion of the property, as it is located within the City of Larkspur.

2. **Tamal Vista West.** This property extends from the High Canal to Tamal Vista Boulevard. The proposal is to amend the General Plan to provide a land use designation with a potential zoning density of up to 10.9 dwelling units per acre. The property owners propose 14 units of single-family, attached housing on this site. The property contains 56,000 square feet and a schematic design has been prepared to show the feasibility of this density. As part of the proposed development on this site, the developer would be required to construct a pedestrian and bicyclist bridge over the high canal to link with the property to the east, which is a de facto trail in Larkspur. This trail, where it traverses the Tamal Vista West site, would be designed and developed by the property owner on a 20-foot pathway easement to be deeded to the Town.

3. Tamal Vista Boulevard right-of-way. The property owners contend that Tamal Vista Boulevard was never deeded to the Town and that fee simple ownership of this land is held by the Porter/Cooley families. The property owners have agreed to deed this right-of-way to the Town and thereby clear any questions which might cloud the title or create legal issues regarding public use of this roadway.

4. Tamal Vista East. This segment provides the opportunity for the Town to develop a needed east-west connection under the freeway. Although the present General Plan designation, "Arterial," would not be changed, the property owners propose to sell the land to the Town at 50 percent of its appraised fair market value, with the total cost to the Town not to exceed $350,000 - an offer extending through December 31, 1991. The Town Council would be the appropriate entity to negotiate this date with the applicants which would be included in a future Development Agreement. The appraisal would be based on these assumptions: Industrial as the allowed land use, Zoning restrictions as contained in the M-Light Industrial District, a 20-foot pathway easement would be deeded to the Town, and the appraisal approach used for condemnation would be used, and there would be no severance damages included in the appraisal. The Town would select and pay for the appraiser. If the property owners disagree with the Town's appraisal, the owners will retain their appraiser; if there is no agreement between the two appraisals, those two appraisers will select a third appraiser as arbiter. After December 31, the "50 percent off" arrangement would expire, and the owners would then add 10 percent of the established price per year thereafter.

This potential for an arterial to connect Redwood Highway and Nellen Avenue to Tamal Vista Boulevard would result in a significant improvement in the Level of Service for several of the most congested intersections in the Town.

5. "Y" shaped parcel. The General Plan land use designation for this piece would be "Office" for the 400 feet approaching Industrial Way. Under this designation, the property owner would be allowed to develop an office building containing 12,000 square feet of gross floor area to be located at the northerly end of the "Y". As part of the overall conceptual parameters of the owners' proposal, the owners will deed to the Town a 20-foot easement for a pathway through this property, and they will commit to develop that pathway on the office parcel as part of that future development. The remainder of the "Y" property would be deeded to the Town, including the holding at the end of Industrial Way which would provide the Town with control over the access from Industrial Way to the north-south alignment of the former Northwestern Pacific Railroad right-of-way.

CEQA STATUS:

Guidelines implementing the California Environmental Quality Act allow the use of an earlier Environmental Impact Report as the environmental document for a subsequent project if the environmental circumstances are essentially the same. The EIR prepared for the General Plan and certified in December 1989 actually considered the land use relationships being proposed at this time. The draft General Plan had proposed the higher density residential development on the west side of Tamal Vista Boulevard, but this proposal was revised during the final hearings on the General Plan. The EIR actually included an evaluation of this higher residential density without finding significant impacts. Also, the EIR contemplated the development of 66,000 square feet of office space at nearby Madera Bay Park. Thus, in a sense, the applicants' potential proposal for this 12,000 square

feet of office development could be substituted because of its much smaller scale.

An initial study has been undertaken for this General Plan amendment, and it shows that conditions in the area have not changed materially since the 1989 EIR was prepared. In preparing the initial study, the overriding concern was what changes have been made since the earlier certified EIR was drafted.

Citizens' review. Public notice of this proposed General Plan Amendment included the notice of staff's and the Planning Commission's recommendation of the earlier EIR as the environmental document for this project. One response was sent which might be interpreted as a comment on the use of this EIR. That response was received from Barbara Salzman, speaking for the Marin Audubon Society, who made the following statement: "It [i.e. the proposed amendment] would set the stage for and, in fact, require that this specific project be carried out by a development agreement, yet the environmental review and analysis of potential impacts has been non-existent." Mrs. Salzman's full letter is attached for your Council's review. A response was given to Mrs. Salzman's letter on the day it was received at the Planning Commission meeting, explaining that a general environmental evaluation has already been given in the earlier EIR; however, a thorough analysis will be required at the time any specific development proposal is submitted.

The 1989 EIR included the same trail system which is proposed by this application. Mrs. Salzman's concerns about the design for such a trail are important concerns but can more easily be addressed once a specific project is proposed. For example, no design has been proposed for the trails; no surface material has been proposed; and no proposals for mitigations have been made.

Recommended CEQA action. Because there have been no material changes brought about by the proposed amendment, it would be appropriate to rely on that EIR for this project, which staff and the Planning Commission are recommending. At the time specific development is proposed, the EIR prepared at that time will focus on specific environmental impacts of the specific development proposal and appropriate mitigation measures for the project.1

1 It should be emphasized that the underlying development proposals described herein are but mere concepts. No development applications, no precise plans, no project plans, and no design plans have been submitted to the Town for consideration.
embedding.first_500_words: June 4, 1992 Deanne Stone, Esq. Legal Division Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Formal Written Advice regarding Councilmember Vaso Medigovich, Town of Corte Madera Official for whom opinion is solicited: Vaso Medigovich, Councilmember of the Town of Corte Madera. Address of Mr. Medigovich: 160 Birch Drive, Corte Madera, CA 94976 Dear Ms. Stone: I am the Town Attorney for the Town of Corte Madera. I have been authorized and requested by Vaso Medigovich, one of the Town's Councilmembers, to solicit this formal written advice from the FPPC. This request is submitted pursuant to 2 Cal. Code Regs. section 18329(b). Factual Statement The Town of Corte Madera is located in the County of Marin. It is a general law city. Its council is made up of five individuals, one of whom is Vaso Medigovich. Mr. Medigovich owns four separate pieces of real property located in the Town: one is his residence and the other three are rental properties which he rents to other individuals. The locations and addresses of these four parcels are shown on the attached, color-coded plan of the Town of Corte Madera. See Exhibit "A". An abandoned railroad right-of-way ("ROW") is located to the north of these four parcels. It is partly within the City of Larkspur and partly in the Town of Corte Madera ("Town"). Its location is shown on Exhibit "A". It is owned by the Porter-Cooley family. jw/vaso1 The Town staff and the Porter-Cooley family have been discussing the uses to which the Porter-Cooley family would like to put this ROW. In a staff report to the Council dated June 11, 1991 (for a council meeting of June 18, 1991) the various parts of this ROW are depicted and a description of the proposed uses for each part is set forth. Please see Exhibit "B", pages 2-3. You will note that in this staff report, reference is made to a general plan amendment. That amendment has already been approved and **is not the subject of this request for advice**. However, as you can appreciate from reading the description of the proposed elements of the arrangement between the Town and Porter-Cooley, a multitude of decisions by the Town Council must be made to effect this proposal. Be mindful, however, that no decisions other than the general plan amendment have been made to approve any of the land use projects described in Exhibit "B". Thus, for example, as to the Tamal Vista West segment of the ROW, in order for the Porter-Cooley family to develop this land into the proposed 14 unit housing development, a subdivision map, preliminary plans, design review approval and perhaps a rezone application, would have to be filed by Porter-Cooley and approved by the Town. Indeed, the landowners may decide that a development agreement is the best vehicle to implement their development scheme, and, naturally, that development agreement would require Council approval. Furthermore, Town purchase of a portion or all of the ROW is or
